channel.title: Oracle on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Oracle on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] ORA-00600: Internal Error
item.description:
ORA-00600: Internal Error

ORA-00600 is an Oracle internal error that indicates a bug in the Oracle kernel code. It is raised when the database encounters an unexpected internal condition that it cannot handle gracefully.

Common Causes

  • A known Oracle bug triggered by specific data patterns or operations
  • Data corruption in tablespaces or data blocks
  • Insufficient resources (memory, file descriptors) causing internal failure
  • Running an Oracle version with known ORA-00600 defects

How to Fix

Check the Trace File

# Trace files are in the dump directory
ls -lt $ORACLE_BASE/diag/rdbms/<db_name>/<instance>/trace/
# Look for files with "ora" in the name

Search for the First Argument

# The error format is: ORA-00600: internal error code, arguments: [arg1], [arg2], ...
# Search Metalink/My Oracle Support for the first argument
# e.g., ORA-00600: [kcbz_check_objd_typ_3]

Apply Latest Patch Set

# Check current version
SELECT * FROM v$version;

# Download and apply latest CPU/PSU from Oracle Support

Check for Block Corruption

-- Run RMAN block check
BACKUP VALIDATE CHECK LOGICAL DATABASE;

-- Check specific tablespace
SELECT * FROM v$database_block_corruption;

Try to Open Database in Restricted Mode

-- If database won't open
STARTUP MOUNT;
ALTER DATABASE OPEN RESTRICTED;

Examples

-- Internal error during query
SELECT * FROM large_table WHERE id = 123;
-- ORA-00600: internal error code, arguments: [kdsgrp1], [], [], [], [], [], [], []
-- Fix: check for block corruption with RMAN

-- Error during DDL
ALTER TABLESPACE users ADD DATAFILE;
-- ORA-00600: internal error code, arguments: [4194], [30], [25], [], []
-- Fix: apply latest patch set

Related Errors

  • ORA-01555 — snapshot too old
  • ORA-04031 — shared memory allocation failure

item.title: [Solution] ORA-01555: Snapshot Too Old
item.description:
ORA-01555: Snapshot Too Old

ORA-01555 occurs when a long-running query needs to read data that has been overwritten in the undo (rollback) tablespace. The undo data needed for consistent read is no longer available.

Common Causes

  • The undo tablespace is too small for the workload
  • A long-running query references data modified by concurrent transactions
  • The UNDO_RETENTION setting is too low
  • The undo tablespace has autoextend disabled

How to Fix

Increase Undo Retention

ALTER SYSTEM SET undo_retention = 3600; -- 1 hour

Resize the Undo Tablespace

ALTER DATABASE DATAFILE '/u01/oradata/undotbs01.dbf' RESIZE 20G;
-- or add a new datafile
ALTER TABLESPACE undotbs1 ADD DATAFILE '/u01/oradata/undotbs02.dbf'
 SIZE 10G AUTOEXTEND ON;

Enable Guaranteed Undo Retention

ALTER TABLESPACE undotbs1 RETENTION GUARANTEE;

Optimize Long-Running Queries

-- Break long queries into smaller batches
-- Instead of:
SELECT * FROM large_table;

-- Use:
SELECT * FROM large_table WHERE id BETWEEN 1 AND 100000;
SELECT * FROM large_table WHERE id BETWEEN 100001 AND 200000;

Check Current Undo Usage

SELECT tablespace_name, SUM(bytes) / 1024 / 1024 AS mb
FROM dba_undo_extents
GROUP BY tablespace_name;

Examples

-- Long-running report
SELECT /*+ PARALLEL(4) */ * FROM sales WHERE quarter = 'Q1';
-- ORA-01555: snapshot too old: rollback segment number 5 with name "_SYSSMU5$"
-- Fix: increase undo_retention or optimize the query

-- Concurrent DML during report
-- Session 1: long query
-- Session 2: DELETE FROM sales WHERE ...
-- Fix: use UNDO_RETENTION GUARANTEE

Related Errors

  • ORA-01652 — temp segment extension failure
  • ORA-08177 — serialization access failure

item.title: [Solution] ORA-01652: Unable to Extend Temp Segment
item.description:
ORA-01652: Unable to Extend Temp Segment

ORA-01652 occurs when Oracle cannot allocate space for a temporary segment in the temporary tablespace. This typically happens during large sorting, hashing, or index creation operations.

Common Causes

  • The temporary tablespace is too small for the operation
  • A query requires a large sort area that exceeds temp space
  • The temporary tablespace datafile cannot auto-extend
  • A runaway query is consuming all available temp space

How to Fix

Check Temporary Tablespace Usage

SELECT
 tablespace_name,
 SUM(bytes_used) / 1024 / 1024 AS used_mb,
 SUM(bytes_free) / 1024 / 1024 AS free_mb
FROM v$temp_space_header
GROUP BY tablespace_name;

Add Datafile to Temporary Tablespace

ALTER TABLESPACE temp ADD TEMPFILE '/u01/oradata/temp02.dbf'
 SIZE 10G AUTOEXTEND ON NEXT 1G MAXSIZE 30G;

Resize Existing Tempfile

ALTER DATABASE TEMPFILE '/u01/oradata/temp01.dbf' RESIZE 20G;

Kill Runaway Sessions

SELECT sid, serial#, sql_id, temp_space_allocated / 1024 / 1024 AS mb
FROM v$rsql
WHERE temp_space_allocated > 100 * 1024 * 1024;

ALTER SYSTEM KILL SESSION 'sid,serial' IMMEDIATE;

Optimize the Query

-- Add indexes to reduce sort operations
CREATE INDEX idx_large ON large_table(sort_column);

-- Use hints to avoid sorting
SELECT /*+ NO_SORT */ * FROM large_table ORDER BY col;

Examples

-- Large sort operation
SELECT * FROM million_row_table ORDER BY created_date;
-- ORA-01652: unable to extend temp segment by 128 in tablespace TEMP
-- Fix: ALTER TABLESPACE temp ADD TEMPFILE ...

-- Index creation
CREATE INDEX idx_big ON huge_table(col);
-- ORA-01652: unable to extend temp segment by 256 in tablespace TEMP
-- Fix: increase temp tablespace size

Related Errors

  • ORA-04031 — shared memory allocation failure
  • ORA-012899 — value too large for column

item.title: [Solution] ORA-04031: Unable to Allocate Shared Memory
item.description:
ORA-04031: Unable to Allocate Shared Memory

ORA-04031 occurs when Oracle cannot allocate a contiguous block of shared memory from the shared pool. The SGA is fragmented or too small for the current workload.

Common Causes

  • The shared pool is too small for the application’s cursor usage
  • Excessive hard parsing due to lack of bind variables
  • Shared pool fragmentation from many small allocations
  • Large PL/SQL packages or cursor caching consuming shared memory

How to Fix

Check Shared Pool Size

SELECT name, value / 1024 / 1024 AS mb
FROM v$sga
WHERE name IN ('Shared Pool', 'Large Pool', 'Java Pool');

Increase Shared Pool Size

ALTER SYSTEM SET shared_pool_size = 4G SCOPE=BOTH;

Flush Shared Pool

-- Temporary fix: flush shared pool
ALTER SYSTEM FLUSH SHARED_POOL;

Use Bind Variables to Reduce Parsing

-- WRONG: hard parse every time
EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ' || 123;

-- CORRECT: bind variables reduce shared pool usage
EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = :1' USING 123;

Enable Automatic Memory Management

ALTER SYSTEM SET sga_target = 16G SCOPE=BOTH;
ALTER SYSTEM SET sga_max_size = 16G SCOPE=SPFILE;

Examples

-- Cursor exhaustion
BEGIN
 FOR i IN 1..100000 LOOP
 EXECUTE IMMEDIATE 'SELECT count(*) FROM tab' || i;
 END LOOP;
END;
-- ORA-04031: unable to allocate 4192 bytes of shared memory

-- Fix: use bind variables and increase shared_pool_size

Related Errors

  • ORA-00600 — internal error
  • ORA-01652 — temp segment extension failure

item.title: [Solution] ORA-08177: Can't Serialize Access
item.description:
ORA-08177: Can’t Serialize Access

ORA-08177 occurs when a serializable transaction attempts to modify data that has been modified by another committed transaction since the serializable transaction started. This is a concurrency conflict at the SERIALIZABLE isolation level.

Common Causes

  • The transaction is running at SERIALIZABLE isolation level
  • Another session modified the same rows between the SELECT and UPDATE
  • High concurrency on the same table rows
  • Long-running serializable transactions increase conflict window

How to Fix

Use READ COMMITTED Isolation Instead

ALTER SESSION SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL READ COMMITTED;

Implement Optimistic Locking

-- Add a version column
ALTER TABLE orders ADD version NUMBER DEFAULT 1;

-- Check version during update
UPDATE orders
SET status = 'shipped', version = version + 1
WHERE id = 100 AND version = 5;

-- If rows updated = 0, another session modified the row

Reduce Transaction Duration

-- Commit more frequently to reduce conflict window
-- Break large transactions into smaller ones

Retry the Transaction

-- In application code, catch ORA-08177 and retry
BEGIN
 -- transaction
 COMMIT;
EXCEPTION
 WHEN OTHERS THEN
 IF SQLCODE = -8177 THEN
 ROLLBACK;
 -- retry after brief wait
 END IF;
END;

Use SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED

-- Avoid blocking by skipping locked rows
SELECT * FROM orders
WHERE status = 'pending'
FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED;

Examples

-- Serializable transaction conflict
SET TRANSACTION ISOLATION LEVEL SERIALIZABLE;
SELECT * FROM inventory WHERE product_id = 1; -- sees qty = 10
-- Another session: UPDATE inventory SET qty = 5 WHERE product_id = 1; COMMIT;
UPDATE inventory SET qty = 8 WHERE product_id = 1;
-- ORA-08177: can't serialize access

Related Errors

  • ORA-01555 — snapshot too old
  • ORA-00600 — internal error

item.title: [Solution] ORA-12899: Value Too Large for Column
item.description:
ORA-12899: Value Too Large for Column

ORA-12899 occurs when an INSERT or UPDATE tries to store a value that is wider than the column’s defined size. Oracle enforces strict column length limits.

Common Causes

  • The value being inserted exceeds the column’s VARCHAR2/CHAR size
  • Multi-byte character encoding requires more bytes than expected
  • The column was defined with a smaller size than required
  • Implicit conversion from a larger type expands the data

How to Fix

Check Column Definitions

SELECT column_name, data_type, data_length, char_length
FROM user_tab_columns
WHERE table_name = 'USERS';

Widen the Column

ALTER TABLE users MODIFY email VARCHAR2(255);

Truncate the Value Before Inserting

INSERT INTO users (name, email)
VALUES ('Alice', SUBSTR('very_long_email@example.com', 1, 100));

Check Character Set Impact

-- AL32UTF8 uses up to 4 bytes per character
SELECT lengthb('café'), length('café') FROM dual;
-- 5 bytes, 4 characters

Use CLOB for Large Values

ALTER TABLE users ADD notes CLOB;

Examples

-- Column too small
INSERT INTO users (email) VALUES ('very.long.email.address@domain.com');
-- ORA-12899: value too large for column "APP"."USERS"."EMAIL" (actual: 30, maximum: 20)

-- Fix: ALTER TABLE users MODIFY email VARCHAR2(100);

-- Multi-byte character issue
INSERT INTO users (name) VALUES ('Übergrößenmaßstab');
-- ORA-12899 in UTF8: actual 38, maximum 20
-- Fix: increase column size to account for multi-byte characters

Related Errors

  • ORA-01652 — temp segment extension failure
  • ORA-01555 — snapshot too old
